name: Colombia
summary: Colombia's digital nomad visa (Visa V) and Medellín's eternal spring climate made it a Latin American favorite — but cost, safety, and visa-renewal mechanics shift fast.

name: Argentina
summary: Argentina's chronic inflation paired with the blue dollar makes Buenos Aires one of the cheapest world-class cities for remote workers willing to navigate parallel exchange rates.

name: El Salvador
summary: Bitcoin as legal tender, a friendly Bitcoin Country freedom visa, and the safest country in Latin America — a unique stack for crypto-native remote workers.

name: Venezuela
summary: Off the standard nomad trail, but the dollarized economy and recovering Caracas tech scene attract a niche crowd. Practical intelligence is hard to find — and worth more here than anywhere.

name: Paraguay
summary: Asunción is the cheapest Latin American capital — sub-$1k/mo budgets are realistic. Paraguay's 10% flat tax and easy residency draw crypto founders and long-haul nomads.

name: Ecuador
summary: Fully dollarized, with a Rentista visa under $1,300/mo and cheap healthcare. Cuenca is the long-term-expat capital; Quito gives big-city access at altitude.

name: Serbia
summary: No formal nomad visa, but a generous 90-day visa-free policy plus a fast Belgrade-based registration route make Serbia a Balkans hub for crypto founders and remote tech workers.

name: Montenegro
summary: Montenegro's digital nomad visa, 9% flat tax route, and Adriatic coast give it a compelling profile — but infrastructure and banking are uneven.

name: Albania
summary: One-year visa-free entry for Americans, low cost of living, and a Unique Permit for nomads. Tirana and Saranda are quietly building out coworking and long-stay infrastructure.

name: Croatia
summary: The Digital Nomad Residence Permit gives a year in the EU on the Adriatic coast. Split and Zagreb have the strongest year-round communities; EUR pricing keeps things honest.

name: North Macedonia
summary: Skopje is the cheapest Balkans-adjacent capital with a freelancer-friendly tax regime. EU-accession track adds slow upside; 1 Gb fiber and a quiet expat community add immediate value.

name: Georgia
summary: 365-day visa-free entry, the Remotely from Georgia program, and crypto-friendly banking make Tbilisi a perennial choice for nomads who want a year of breathing room.

name: Uzbekistan
summary: Tashkent's emerging tech scene and visa-free 30-day entry for many Western passports make Uzbekistan the most accessible Central Asian gateway for short remote stints.

name: Kazakhstan
summary: Almaty's coworking scene, strong mobile data, and a 30-day visa-free policy give Kazakhstan the strongest Central Asia infrastructure stack outside the Caspian capitals.

name: Kyrgyzstan
summary: 60-day visa-free entry, Issyk-Kul's summer scene, and Bishkek's rock-bottom rents make Kyrgyzstan the cheapest Central Asian option for remote workers.

name: Mongolia
summary: Ulaanbaatar is the most remote big-city option for nomads. 30-day visa-free for most Western passports, fast 5G, sub-$1k/mo. Pure frontier with a steppe escape on the doorstep.

name: Vietnam
summary: New 90-day e-visa with multiple entries opened Vietnam meaningfully to nomads — Da Nang and Hoi An have the strongest secondary infrastructure outside Saigon.

name: Thailand
summary: The Destination Thailand Visa (DTV) — 180 days entry, 5-year validity — changed the calculus for nomads. Secondary cities like Chiang Rai and Pai have far weaker SERPs than Chiang Mai.

name: Indonesia
summary: Indonesia's E33G remote worker KITAS gives a year of legal nomad residence. Alternative Bali areas like Sidemen, Amed, and Lovina are where the next wave is moving as Canggu prices climb.

name: Malaysia
summary: DE Rantau Nomad Pass and Penang's English-speaking food scene make Malaysia the easiest soft landing in Southeast Asia for first-time nomads. Stronger fiber than its neighbors.

name: Barbados
summary: The 12-month Welcome Stamp gives remote workers tax-free foreign income, fast broadband, and a community already full of remote tech workers. The most-trodden Caribbean nomad path.

name: Dominica
summary: The Work In Nature (WIN) Extended Stay Visa runs 18 months. DCash CBDC, rainforest interior, and very little tourist saturation make Dominica the Caribbean's most undercovered nomad pick.

name: Mauritius
summary: The Premium Visa is free, lasts a year, and asks only $1,500/mo. Foreign income isn't taxed locally and the island has the strongest infrastructure stack in Africa.

name: Namibia
summary: Namibia's Digital Nomad Visa runs six months at $2,000/mo. English is official, the dollar pegs to ZAR, and Windhoek has serious coworking infrastructure for the region.

name: Cape Verde
summary: Atlantic island chain off West Africa with a Remote Working Program for EU/US passports. Year-round trade winds, decent fiber in São Vicente, Portuguese-speaking, EUR-pegged escudo.

name: Kenya
summary: Kenya's Class N Digital Nomad permit launched in 2025. Nairobi's Silicon Savannah tech scene plus Mombasa for coast time. English official, undercovered, and improving fast.
